page_content: - a) fires - b) floods - c) famines - d) the spread of an epidemic - e) the breakout of war or external aggression - f) internal disorders like communal riots, struggle between various classes, castes and sects and other clashe
- a) proper handling of the records - b) upkeep and maintenance of the records - c) preparation, destruction, revision and modification of the records of various police units

- a) police has to take measures to free flow of the traffic. - b) proper enforcement work shall be taken. - c) traffic awareness meeting shall be conducted. - d) necessary suggestions shall be given to the r&b department for take up road widening and improvements.

- a) the education department during examinations, students, and employees strikes and other situations of disorder, - b) the revenue department and loaning organizations for recovery of loans revenue collection etc., - c) the departments like banks and municipalities for guards etc for the removal of encroachments etc, and - d) the other departments as and when the need arises.

- a) police are the most visible and effective pros of the police department. they should thereof understand the importance of public relations and its present state in the area of their operations and functions. - b) the officer and the staff must try to improve public relations and police image.

the various ranks, functionaries and positions of the police organisation can be classified broadly into two categories i.e. gazetted officers and nongazetted officers or junior officers. the gazetted and the senior/superior officers, who belong to the ips or state service cadres function in the supervisory capacity, whereas the junior/subordinate officers work as field officers and most of the police functions and duties are performed by them in person with the assistance of other ranks. the junior/subordinate officers are known as inspectors/sub-inspectors, assistant subinspectors, head constables and constables. the powers, functions, role and duties of these officers are as mentioned below.

an inspector of police works in the capacity of a circle inspector, sho of a police station, staff officer or as part of some other unit of the police organisation. his functions and duties as circle inspector or in various capacities are...

1. the main duties of circle inspector of police are investigation of important cases. 2. supervision of police work of his circle and branches, 3. maintain discipline among subordinates, 4. keep the superintendent of police and the sub divisional police officer informed of the state of circle, its crime and law and order
situation on a regular basis from time to time.

1. the inspector must thoroughly inspect each ps of his circle once in a year, should constantly and systematically tour throughout his circle. he should make inquiries about bad characters, the state of crime, community policing and general efficiency of police work. 2. he should visit each of his police stations once in a month, check the current work of the station, and give necessary instructions, pointing out errors, entering the same in the visitors' note book and sending a copy to the sdpo. he shall enter in the visitors' book the details of cases in which instructions issued relating to delay in investigation, finalization, and trials. 3. he shall discuss with the sub-inspectors and ios, the cases under investigation, clues for detection and assist in the progress of the cases in finalizing them without unnecessary delay. 4. he should also supervise the prosecutions of cases, for quick disposal, and see that the witnesses are produced in courts regularly and coordinate with prosecutors and enlist co-ordination in the success of cases. 5. during his visits to the police stations, he shall scrutinize the station crime history and see whether all cases have been entered correctly and the register is maintained properly. he should peruse duty roster to see whether beats are properly detailed and checked. he should also check beats himself and visit as many villages as possible and see that he visits all villages once in a year. the villages visited by the inspector shall be noted in the village roster maintained by him. the sdpo must watch the progress of village visits by the inspector. 6. the inspector must inspect twice a year, the books, shops, premises and stocks of all licensed manufactures and vendors of arms, ammunition and military stores. he shall also inspect all licensed revolvers/pistols at the licensee's residence in the first quarter of each year.

1. the inspector shall assume charge of investigation in all grave crimes and be responsible from the initial stages till the finalization and prosecution of the cases. 2. he shall personally investigate any other important case, or if the sdpo or superintendent of police orders him to do so. he shall write the case diaries in all cases that are investigated by him.

3. on the occurrence of a crime of any importance, the inspector should proceed to the spot without delay, to supervise and guide the investigation and take charge of the direction and co-ordination of all work. 4. where the inspector takes up the investigation, he shall continue till it takes a definite shape. if however he has to leave the investigation for some unavoidable reasons, to be recorded in writing, he must come back as soon as possible and continue the investigation till the case takes a definite shape. in case he has to leave a case during investigation he should record in his case diary the opinion he has formed of the cases so far as well as detailed instructions regarding the line of further action. 5. whenever the inspector is present at the investigation made by one of his subordinates, he shall countersign the case diary certifying its correctness. this does not however absolve the sub-inspector of his primary responsibility of investigation of the case properly.

metadata.subject: police_administration
metadata.heading: duties at the time of breach of peace: (pso-92)

page_content: 1. if a serious breach of the peace is anticipated the inspector shall promptly proceed to the locality and take measures to prevent it. in the absence of any senior officer, he shall take command of the police engaged in suppressing any riot taking place in his presence. he should take the help of public i.e. maithri committees, peace committees, youth committees which are available. 2. the inspector should be present on the occasion of large festivals and public assemblies and supervise the police arrangements. 3. he should plan, co-ordinate and organize anti-terrorist operations by collecting useful information about terrorists, their hide outs, shelters, financiers, modules etc.

metadata.subject: police_administration
metadata.heading: submitting of weekly diary and reports: (pso-93)

page_content: 1. at the close of the week, the inspector shall submit a weekly diary. he should enter in it the work done by him and any other matter of departmental interest. when the inspector leaves his headquarters, his halting places and the village visited, the distance traveled and the manner of performing journey should be given. the weekly diary should be dispatched on monday containing the details up to previous saturday night. if anything of importance has occurred on sunday or in a journey undertaken by him on sunday, the same also will be incorporated in the diary. 2. besides the weekly diaries, the inspector must also send special and immediate reports on any important or urgent matters, which occur, in

metadata.subject: police_administration
metadata.heading: submitting of weekly diary and reports: (pso-93)

page_content: his circle.

metadata.subject: police_administration
metadata.heading: submitting of weekly diary and reports: (pso-93)

page_content: 3. he shall also send a monthly itinerary report also. 4. the inspector shall forward to his immediate superior case diaries in cases investigated by him. he shall send a copy of the case diary to the station where the crime is registered and retain one copy with himself.

1. all cognizable cases under the ipc and also cases under special and local laws shall be entered in the inspector's crime register (form 7) to enable him to maintain a check over their investigation and disposal either by himself or station house officers. entry shall be made in column (6) of the points, which the inspector wishes to keep in mind concerning the case. 2. in case in which the inspector does not forward the case diaries to the sdpos as in order no. 60-4 the inspector will write 'f' or 'r' as the case may be below the date, as shown below.
15/2/f:to denote that the case diary dated 15/2 has been filed.
15/2/r:to denote that the case diary dated 15/2 has been returned with remarks to the investigating officer.

3. no such register need be maintained by inspector who is sho of a police station. the case diaries of cases investigated by them shall be sent to acp/sdpo who will maintain the necessary registers. 4. the inspector shall transmit to his immediate superior (acp/sdpo) case diaries received from his subordinates and others who are authorized to investigate except those in cases mentioned in order 604. all case diaries and correspondence relating to each case should be maintained and held in the personal custody of investigating officer and kept in the police station concerned. it is a privileged document and under no circumstances can it be shown to any person other than immediate superior and as provided by law.

the inspector shall record his daily movements and activities chronologically in his note book. when a note book is finished, it shall be filed with the circle records and retained for 3 years. he shall leave his current note book behind when he is transferred.

the inspector shall take care that the circle information book (form 12) is properly compiled and kept up to date. the objective of this book is to pass on the knowledge gained by an officer to his successor.
the inspector shall also record in the circle information book the dates of changes of circle charge.

the inspector must himself compile the annual review of crime and investigation of each station in part-i of the station crime history. he shall send it to the acp/sdpo for approval before end of january. after approval he will send it to the station keeping a copy in his office for his use. he should also see that entries in part-iv of station crime history are properly made. he should enter in his circle information book a gist of the entries made in the part-iv of the station crime history of each station.

1. the circle inspector should record his remarks on the inspection of a station in the inspectors visiting book (form 13) and forward a carbon copy to his immediate superior with his diary. 2. the inspector must check guards and attend drill in his head quarters station as frequently as possible and in other stations during his visits and inspections, effect meetings with inspectors of border circles and also conduct prosecution in courts, permissible under rules, in simple cases and more particularly in security cases.
the circle inspectors, if present at a place where the collector is camping, should make it a point to call on him.

1. station and circle records, which have to be retained in the district police office, will be sent to the district police office by the inspector in the month of january every year. 2. at the close of each year the inspector will prepare and forward to the district police office for the orders of the superintendent of police a list in respect of each station, of time expired records, which need not be retained, and other useless papers for destruction. these will be destroyed by the inspector himself on receipt of the orders from the superintendent of police. 3. the inspector is responsible for the destruction of the case diary files and first information reports retained in his office and in stations. these files will be retained for three calendar years after the year in which the case is disposed off. if a case remains undisposed or not fully disposed, case diary file should be retained for a period of 30
years.

4. every inspector, reserve inspector, sub-inspector, reserve subinspector, head constable and constable (not belonging to hqs units) should report his arrival and departure to/from district hq. at the district police office/district control room, unless there is specific reason, like attending court or leaving the hqs within a short time.

metadata.subject: police_administration
metadata.heading: attendance at sessions and other courts: (pso-99)

page_content: as an investigating officer, the inspector should as a rule attend session and the courts of judicial first class magistrates during the trial of the case, unless there are specific reasons for his absence. in such an event he should depute his sub-inspector who assisted him in the investigation. as investigating officer he must assist the prosecution by causing regular attendance of witnesses, before prosecutor for refreshing their memory and for preparation of cross examination, arguments etc. the investigating officer should enter the progress of cases in trials in the court case diaries. the court case diaries should contain a gist of all the proceedings of the day in the court including observations or comments, arguments, presence or otherwise of witnesses, accused, reasons for adjournments etc.

it shall be the duty of the sho to use his best endeavors and ability to prevent crimes, preserve peace, apprehend disorderly and suspicious characters, to investigate and detect offences, bring the offenders to justice, collect and communicate intelligence effecting public peace and promptly obey and execute all orders lawfully issued. the important powers and duties of sub-inspector of police as station house officer are...

- a) the sub-inspector in charge of a police station is fully responsible for the police administration of his charge. the sub-inspector is an important and vital functionary in the police department. - b) he shall be responsible for proper management of the station and optimum utilization of the resources and facilities available. - c) it shall be his duty to manage the staff and work of the police station in accordance with the law and rules and to make the police station a place where the needy public gets necessary and immediate response. - d) the image of the police department basically reflects on the conduct and behaviour of the sub- inspector and his staff in the station, as it is at that point, public has a direct access with the police. - e) the sub-inspector and his staff should behave politely and courteously with the public giving an impression of friendly approach.
active co-operation from citizen committees shall be solicited in the matters of l&o, crime prevention etc.

- f) all illegal methods or ill treatments against persons should be avoided at all costs giving way to an impression to the public that the police are there to extend their helping hand in the discharge of their duties. - g) the sub-inspector must respect human values and human dignity and should know that powers are vested in him to discharge his duties legitimately but not to arrogate to satisfy his ego and vanity. if he crosses his limits he will be doing so at his risk as the department will not come to his rescue. - h) he should assign duties to his staff and personally ensure that these duties are correctly performed. - i) he shall take measures for the prevention of crime, for the preservation of peace, for speedy investigation and prosecution of cases. - j) when more than one sub-inspector is employed in a police station, which is headed by a sub- inspector, the junior officer shall act in accordance with the orders of seniority. - k) he shall maintain a notebook in which he will write then and there his daily movements and activities in discharge of his duties. the completed notebook will be retained for 3 years with the station records. he will hand over his current notebook to his successor when he goes on transfer. - l) he shall acquire full local knowledge and will become acquainted with the people of his station limits, especially the village secretaries and panchayat officials, and will also enlist their co-operation in prevention of crime and breach of peace. he should also involve various committees in this regard. - m) the sho shall normally visit all the villages and localities in towns in his jurisdiction once a quarter. passing through a village without doing the work cannot be treated as a visit.

metadata.subject: police_administration
metadata.heading: duties with regard to law and order & crime investigation: (pso-102):

page_content: the duties of officer in charge of a police station are set out in the police acts and crpc. they broadly relate to maintenance of law and order, prevention and detection of crime, investigation and prosecution of offences etc.
1. the sub inspector of police/sho is responsible for the maintenance of the law and order and public order in his jurisdiction.

2. his duties in this regard, include personal leadership and supervision of duties and work of all the staff and men allotted to his charge including their welfare, discipline and morale. 3. organizing active participation in crime prevention and detection, maintenance of law and order and anti-terror operations, dispersal of unlawful assemblies and prevention of breach of peace and public order. 4. organizing beats and patrols and its personal supervision. 5. designing and implementation of policing schemes suited to such beat areas and securing co-operation of public and maithri/social committees. 6. obtaining prompt information about activities in the field of communal, caste, political, student, or other segments of society including sub- versive elements and identify trouble spots and trouble mongers and to take effective preventive measures like launching security proceed- ings under cr.p.c. 7. maintenance of good public relations in his station limits. 8. visiting and spending adequate time in the villages, localities of his station limit,. 9. maintenance of effective surveillance over bad characters, anti-social elements, and rowdies of the area under his charge. 10. he shall constantly endeavour to maintain high professional standard and keep himself updated, well informed and motivated. 11. keep a pursuit and apprehension of offenders on available clues and information. 12. collection of information on all important matters relevant to police duties and functions and communicating information to his immediate superior police officer and to other concerned superior police officers promptly.

1. the sub inspector of police/sho is responsible for the investigation of all cases reported in his jurisdiction except for those where the investigation is entrusted to inspector/dsp or other senior officers or cid. as officer in charge of police station he shall perform the duties relating to investigation and prosecution of cases. 2. he shall register all cognizable cases and would furnish a copy to the complainant invariably free of cost and send the original fir to the courts concerned immediately and copies to his superior officer.
failure to give a copy of fir to complainant free of cost shall amount to misconduct.

3. in respect of non-cognizable offences reported in the station, he shall record the information in the general diary and also in the noncognizable register, and refer the complainant to the court by an endorsement. 4. in cases of non-cognizable offences referred by court or any cognizable offences presented by parties directly in courts but endorsed by the court to the sho, he shall register such cases and investigate. 5. he shall promptly arrive at the scene of crime and thoroughly examine the same for clues and for other matters of investigation. 6. he shall make arrangements for the preservation of the scene. 7. he shall promptly con- duct investigation by identifying, preserving, collecting and forwarding of material to scientific experts, and collection of documentary and oral evidence. 8. he shall make use of scientific methods available for investigation. 9. he shall hold inquest where necessary and forward the bodies for the post mortem examination according to the necessity. 10. he shall examine witnesses and record their statements accurately and promptly. 11. conduct searches, and seize material, if any, as per provisions of cr.p.c.
arrest persons, where necessary observing the necessary provisions
of law and the decisions of the court and forward them to concerned
court with remand report in time.

13. forward to the court the weapons and article seized from the accused, as well as, seizure affected from searches along with necessary reports. 14. he has to supervise investigation conducted by the subordinate officers (ios) and shall obtain investigation reports. 15. he shall write the case diaries regularly and forward them to the superior officers promptly to enable them to give instructions for further investigations. 16. he shall finalize the cases with- out delay and file charge sheets where the evidence is sufficient for prosecution within the period of limitation imposed by the cr.p.c. and courts.

17. he shall promptly serve and would ensure service of summons and execute warrants. 18. he shall take all steps to promptly prosecute the cases where evidence is sufficient by assisting the prosecutor by producing witnesses promptly to refresh their memory before examine them as witnesses in courts. 19. obtain copies of judgments in cases of acquittal, read them and forward them to the prosecutor for his opinion for preferring appeal.

metadata.subject: police_administration
metadata.heading: inspection of drill and kit of subordinates, training and instructions :

page_content: he shall inspect the drill of his subordinates, hold inspection of their kits, catechize them upon the performance of their duty and examine their beat books and note books.

metadata.subject: police_administration
metadata.heading: inspection of drill and kit of subordinates, training and instructions :

page_content: the police men of the station should be briefed on all matters that have bearing on their work and conduct through written material, television, video and other visual aids, radio programmes specially broadcast and also personal instructions so that they are psychologically oriented to discharge their allotted duties in an efficient manner.

metadata.subject: police_administration
metadata.heading: daily reports from station house officer : (pso-103)

page_content: the station house officer shall forward daily to the inspector a carbon or photo stat copy of the station general diary with a docket in the form prescribed along with copies of f.i.r. registered and case diaries of that day.

1. station house officers of all branches will be supplied with punishment roll forms and when he deals with any officer for punishment he will attach a punishment roll entering in relevant column titled 'defaulters' the total number of defaults and the particulars of the last three punishments. 2. he will forward it to the sdpo who will fill up the first column and transmit the roll to the concerned superintendent of police. the authorities competent to impose penalties are dealt with in the chapter titled 'disciplinary proceedings, punishments and appeals'. 3. when an inspector or station house officer calls for an explanation from any subordinate officer, it should be obtained and submitted at once to the sdpo accompanied by a punishment roll unless reasons are furnished for non-submission of a punishment roll.

metadata.subject: police_administration
metadata.heading: non-appearance of pensioners to draw pension:

page_content: on receipt of information regarding non-appearance of pensioner for over 3 months enquiry may be caused by the police and the concerned officer

1. inspectors, and sub-inspectors make authorised entries in the small service books of their subordinates. it is their responsibility to keep them updated. 2. entries relating to biennial medical examination, the ailment and treatment undergone and all relevant information relating to the health of the constable and the head constable should be entered and shown to him. 3. important instructions given and the manner in which the tasks were performed, the reports if any concerning any constable may also be entered after bringing it to the notice of the sdpo. 4. the small service book should be a complete record of the service including minor and major punishments, rewards, commendation letters, leave and performance of the constable and should therefore be maintained in a regular manner. it shall be the duty of the s.h.o. to ensure this.

metadata.subject: police_administration
metadata.heading: power and duties of sub-inspector of police as station house officer of traffic police station: (pso-104)

page_content: 1. the station house officer of traffic police station should acquire full knowledge of all roads, highways and congested areas. 2. he should also acquire knowledge of the details of the vehicles, density of traffic, movement of contraband and suspected persons, road side truck terminals, parking places, eating houses, motels etc. 3. keeping in view the specific problems of the areas he should make arrangements for the removal of obstructions, clearing traffic jams, observance of traffic regulations, rendering first aid and prompt medical assistance to road accident victims, incidence control and immediate action to preserve the scene of the road accidents. 4. he shall maintain close liaison with the officials and engineers of roads and transport department, local bodies, electricity, telephones etc., and take short as well as long-term measures for smooth flow of traffic. 5. he shall pay attention to the training of his staff and actively participate in spreading traffic education in his area. 6. he shall also render free and social aid to all victims or their representatives in claiming compensation in compensatory tribunals by furnishing the copies of f.i.r., inquest report p.m. report or wound certificate, copy of insurance certificate free of cost as well as

metadata.subject: police_administration
metadata.heading: power and duties of sub-inspector of police as station house officer of traffic police station: (pso-104)

page_content: by advising them to that end.

metadata.subject: police_administration
metadata.heading: power and duties of sub-inspector of police as station house officer of traffic police station: (pso-104)

page_content: 7. patrolling of highways is one of his important duties. the matters that should be attended to in each area or beat have to be identified and instructions issued in the patrol register. 8. maintenance of patrol vehicles and equipment to keep them in top condition is his responsibility.

metadata.subject: police_administration
metadata.heading: powers and duties of sub inspectors of railway police as sho of railway police station: (pso-105)

page_content: 1. the sub-inspectors of railway police shall perform the duties as local police. 2. they shall acquaint themselves with the functioning of the railway, the details of all trains that pass through. 3. the nature of crime that occur, the criminals or groups that operates in railways, the prevention of thefts. 4. maintenance of order on platform, ticket windows, parcel offices, cloak rooms, marshalling yards, the organisation of beats and surveillance, security arrangements, and action to be initiated in case of accidents.

metadata.subject: police_administration
metadata.heading: powers and duties of sub inspectors of railway police as sho of railway police station: (pso-105)

metadata.subject: police_administration
metadata.heading: the following shall be the duties of the assistant sub-inspector.

page_content: 1. investigation of simple cases as directed by sho and assisting investigation in all cases handled by shos and other superior officers. 2. petition enquiry. 3. checking of enquiries in verification rolls. 4. supervision of service of process work. 5. to function as an officer in charge of a beat area and check patrols. 6. direct supervision or handling of station script work. 7. detailing duties to the staff during sub-inspectors absence and exercising supervision and control over the constables and head constables. 8. maintenance of cash book. 9. preparation of pay and traveling allowance bills (he should sign them only when the si is absent). 10. maintenance of arms and ammunition and registers connected therewith. 11. to be in charge of out-post or beat and perform these duties when posted there. 12. such other work as may be entrusted to him.

metadata.subject: police_administration
metadata.heading: duties of the general duty head constable:

page_content: head constables are mainly employed to be in charge of general duty in police stations, as station writer, as officer in charge of out post and guards, armed reserves, in charge of beat areas in rural and town police stations. he acts as sho in the absence of sub-inspector and asst. sub-inspector. he is authorised to hold inquests and make investigation when asked by the sub inspector to do so.

metadata.subject: police_administration
metadata.heading: duties of the general duty head constable:

page_content: - a) he will work with constables and help them to understand instructions, catechism and drill and help them. - b) to perform duties allotted by sho. - c) he shall be in charge of guard or escort when deputed.

metadata.subject: police_administration
metadata.heading: duties of the general duty head constable:

page_content: - d) he shall visit villages in the station jurisdiction when deputed for a specific purpose. - e) to work as hc in charge of a particular beat area. - f) he will attend to court work under the orders of sho. - g) to investigate cases when deputed by the sho and to assist the io in investigation. - h) to conduct enquiries into petty complaints. - i) to take care of arrested persons kept in police station. - j) he will take care of reception and proper behaviour with the persons coming to police station and to attend telephone calls. - k) to be present in the police station in the absence of sub-inspector unless called away in an emergency. he should take approval of s.i before leaving the station on routine duty.

metadata.subject: police_administration
metadata.heading: duties of the head constable as station writer: (pso-108 )

page_content: 1. head constable shall normally be assigned the duties of station writer. he will perform the ministerial work of the station under the direction of sho. 2. in the absence of si or asi, he will allocate urgent duties to the constables and be in charge of station property including arms and ammunition and carry out the routine work of the station. 3. in the absence of the si, asi and other senior, hc, he will function as station house officer; take steps for registration and investigation of cases reported under his charge. 4. he may also be employed by sho to go on night rounds and may occasionally be kept in charge of beat area. 5. he is also responsible to watch the arrested persons kept in police station and proper reception to persons visiting police station and also for answering telephone calls when sho, si, or asi are not present in police station.

metadata.subject: police_administration
metadata.heading: duties of head constable as investigation assistant :

page_content: the head constables form the main strength of investigating team. they assist the team leader in the investigation of cases especially in preserving, and collecting the clues like finger prints, foot prints, materials, photograph etc. and also in...

metadata.subject: police_administration
metadata.heading: duties of head constable as investigation assistant :

page_content: 1. collection of information, 2. process service,

metadata.subject: police_administration
metadata.heading: duties of head constable as investigation assistant :

page_content: 3. pursuit of clues and arrest of accused and recovery of property, 4. assisting in searches and seizures, 5. surveillance of specially marked persons, 6. holding inquests where directed, 7. securing the presence of witnesses, 8. court duty to assist prosecutor when si or inspector are not able to be present, 9. any other work connected with the investigation of crimes. 10. submitting reports to sho on enquiries made by them.

metadata.subject: police_administration
metadata.heading: duties of head constable as officer in charge of beat:

page_content: head constable or asst. sub-inspector is generally assigned a beat approximately covering 2000 households and around 10,000 populations in cities and towns and a group of villages in rural areas. he shall, with the help of constables provided, be responsible in that area for...

metadata.subject: police_administration
metadata.heading: duties of head constable as officer in charge of beat:

page_content: 1. prevention of all offences, 2. surveillance over criminals and bad characters, 3. collection of information and intelligence, affecting crime, security and law and order, 4. service of beats by night and day, 5. preventing and dealing with public nuisances, maintaining order and incidence control, 6. associate with citizen committees and keeping the sho informed of all happenings, 7. ensure discipline and conduct of constables of his charge, and conducting such enquiries as are entrusted to him.

metadata.subject: police_administration
metadata.heading: duties of head constable as in charge of outpost: (pso-109)

page_content: 1. hc may be kept in charge of small and rural out post. the duty of out-post in charge is to supervise the work of constables in his charge, see to the proper performance of all duties attached to the out-post and maintain the prescribed records. he should submit a copy of the out-post general diary daily to the mother police station. 2. the out-post in-charge is not sho. therefore whenever the information of cognizable offence is received in the out-post, he shall forward it to the mother station for registration of fir. after issuing a receipt for the same to the complainant. he will also enter the substance of the report in the out-post general diary. however the

metadata.subject: police_administration
metadata.heading: duties of head constable as in charge of outpost: (pso-109)

page_content: complainant is entitled for a copy of the fir from the mother police station free of cost.

metadata.subject: police_administration
metadata.heading: duties of head constable as in charge of outpost: (pso-109)

page_content: 3. the officer in charge of an out-post may without intervention of the officer in charge of a police station, take action in such of the offences as can be legally taken by si, asi or hc as per the rank of officer-in-charge. 4. the officer in charge of out-post shall on receipt of a complaint or information of a crime or serious occurrence, take such immediate action as may appear necessary i.e. proceeding and preserving the scene, rendering medical assistance to victims, arrest of accused persons etc. 5. men on duty in outpost should be treated on the same lines as constables allotted to beat duty as far as tenure is concerned. however, a district order should make transfer of men to or from an outpost.

metadata.subject: police_administration
metadata.heading: duties of head constable as station charge: (pso-110)

page_content: in the absence of the regular sho, the senior officer present shall assume charge of the station. senior constable present shall also be deemed to be the officer in charge in the absence of higher ranks as per section 2(o) of cr.p.c.

metadata.subject: police_administration
metadata.heading: duties of head constable as guard incharge: (pso-110)

page_content: 1. whenever a guard is detailed for treasury or jail or for security of a minister of a government, a head constable shall be kept in charge of the guard. 2. a head constable from reserve police will be deputed to be at the disposal of sessions judge, to maintain order in the court when there is a criminal trial. 3. wherever it is warranted, the sho shall make security arrangements commensurate with the situation with the permission and consultation of the district and sessions judge or other judicial officers.

a constable has maximum interface with public. as the most accessible person for public, he is expected to protect the needy, rescue people from danger, apprehend offenders and assist in securing prompt help and justice. some of the important roles assigned to him are organizing and securing community participation, activising himself in prevention and detection of crime and maintenance of law and order. another main role of the constable is, performance of all tasks connected with beat area and there by help in prevention of offence and breach of peace. the constable on traffic duty has the task of regulating traffic. all constables in their dealings with public should inspire confindence in the efficacy of police to protect them. the police image is directly proportionate to their good conduct and behaviour in the public as they are the persons who are basically and directly in touch with them. at all costs they must avoid ill treatment either to the victims or to the accused as a first step to build the better police image.
the main duties of civil constables are;

1. to perform duties in beats, patrols and pickets. 2. to keep surveillance over history sheeted and other potential criminals as per orders 3. collection of information and intelligence relating to crimes and criminals, subversive, terrorist and anti social elements in their areas primarily and communication of the same to the authorized superiors 4. developing cordial relations with local citizen committees/voluntary organizations and knowledge of households in the beat area. 5. convey information relating to persons and events that cause or likely to cause law and order situation or wide spread disturbances 6. keep in touch with local disputes, caste/communal overtones and inform sho 7. assistance to investigating officers in the matter of arrests, recoveries, searches, identification and securing of witnesses or verification of information. 8. execute warrants and serve summons promptly. 9. eescort prisoners, arrested persons, escort injured or dead to the hospital. 10. guard of prisoners in custody and all station property. 11. help and assist in dealing with floods, earthquakes, fires, accidents, epidemics etc. and put in responsible efforts to save lives and property. 12. to perform allotted duties in fairs, festivals, bundhs, agitations, riots, large assemblies, elections, bandobust and security duties. 13. to preserve and guard the scene of occurrence until necessity ceases. 14. to behave courteously with all sections of public and treat poor people, children, women, aged and all weaker sections of society with
consideration, sympathy and helpful attitude.

15. to be regular and punctual in his duties, catechism, physical training and weekly parades. 16. to work as data entry operators in computers and in the areas of reprography, photography and cartography wherever they have necessary skill. 17. make entries in the prescribed register and forms and maintain records entrusted to him particularly those relating to beat area. 18. to meet the common people in his beat on a regular basis and maintain rapport with the public representatives. 19. to visit the victims of crime and keep them updated about the progress of investigation of their cases as per the instructions of the sho, except where the identity of the victims needs to be kept secret or where the victim wants no interference. 20. to keep a list of senior citizens living alone and visit them occasionally to assist them and to prevent crime against them.. 21. to keep a list of vital installations and places of worship and maintain watch over the same. 22. to keep a track of any brewing social, religious or sectarian conflict/unrest. 23. to keep a watch on the movement of foreigners in his area. 24. any other duties allotted by sho or other superior officers. 25. every officer who is supplied with kit at government cost is responsible for keeping it in good condition, and shall be required to replace at his own cost any articles carelessly lost or damaged.

the entire preventive work of the police depends on the efficiency, commitment, professionalism and integrity of the beat area policeman. the constable allotted to a beat should realize that his participation in the community even in a small measure is essential for successful policing. professionalism and integrity but not power and authority shall command respect and bring in a large measure of co-operation from general public.
'people change for two reasons, either their minds have been opened or their hearts have been broken'
- buddha

metadata.subject: police_administration
metadata.heading: 24 hours routine duties in police station:

page_content: the legal obligation of 24 hours deemed to be on duty should not be interpreted to mean that every police person can be on duty indefinitely for days together for any length of time. the following guidelines should be borne in mind on this subject.

metadata.subject: police_administration
metadata.heading: 24 hours routine duties in police station:

page_content: - a) police as a body is on duty all the time, a police officer who is off duty has certain obligations. all police officers are obliged to forego weekly off and work longer than 8 hours when there is a serious emergency. for example if, an officer leaves a task on hand abruptly at the end of 8 hours shift it will result in serious prejudice to a case or injury to persons or disappearance of evidence, continuation of disorder etc.; - b) all police work deals with emergencies in a way. hence normal duties should not be interpreted as emergencies warranting mobilization. inadequacy of man power at times may be due to absence of advance planning, or adequate intelligence. if this is rectified, things can be managed in time with available man power. the responsibility to ensure that emergency mobilization is kept at a bare minimum shall rest with sho, sdpo and sp/dcp.

to keep the personnel well trained, up-dated and physically fit the following schedule for physical exercises, drill and instructional classes is prescribed for all the police stations. the weekly routine in all police stations shall be in general as mentioned below.

briefing & catechism, local information including on job training and relevant orders of police manual. a typical schedule should consist of reading and explaining a chapter or a part of it, all important circulars, and exchange of information on crimes, absconders, arrests and properties.

physical training, weapon training and drill as per module designed for police stations by the training branch. if one of the days assigned for search proceedings and list have legal sanctions under sections 100 and 165 cr.p.c. if he finds no sufficient grounds to justify the forwarding of the accused who is in custody, to the magistrate, shall release him after executing a bond with or without sureties. bail bonds should also be executed for arrested persons on bailable warrants issued by courts.

the case property seized by the investigating officer during the course of the investigation shall be sent to court without delay in form 60, which should be written in triplicate. if the property is seized from the possession of the arrested after due search, as indicated in see.51 cr.p.c, the same should be recorded in form 59.

all arrested persons who were not released on bail shall be searched immediately after arrest to ensure that no contraband or weapon is possessed by them. the woman prisoners should be searched only by another female to keep up the decency. a search of arrested persons has sanction under section 51 cr.p.c. every prisoner, when admitted to lock-up shall be searched and a list of the property, except the wearing apparel retained by the prisoner, shall be entered in the 'prisoners search register' which shall be signed by the senior police officer, the station writer and the prisoner. the description of the prisoner shall also be recorded in search register. all property found on the persons except the wearing apparel shall be sent to the magistrate with form 60.

under sec.436 cr.p.c any person who was arrested by a station house officer for committing an offence for which he is entitled to be released on bail shall be released on bail after taking bail bond, with or without sureties. the bail bond should be written according to form 63.

under section 173 cr.p.c an officer-in-charge of a police station, after completion of the investigation shall send a final report in form no.69 to the magistrate having territorial jurisdiction of the offence. the report must include the details of investigation from the receiving of report/petition to the findings of the investigation.

it is the duty of the station house officer to maintain a process register in form 98 in which shall be entered all processes received from courts for service or execution and the disposal of such processes. the inspector-incharge of the circle shall, once in two months, compare the register, with the register maintained in the court and report any serious delays or omissions to the superintendent of police for taking necessary action. unexecuted processes shall be brought forward in the register at the beginning of the month. a monthly abstract in form 99 shall be entered at the end of the month. as it is the duty of the police to serve processes, laid down in sec.62 and 74 cr.p.c, the station house officer should take utmost care on process work and maintenance of the process register. this aspect of service of process should not be neglected by the sho as it has got bearing on the progress of cases during the trial in the courts. there are complaints, in general from magistrates stating, that the shos are not giving much importance to process work which is vital for the progress of the cases in courts.

after recording the statements of witnesses u/s 161 cr.p.c, the investigating officer directs the complainant and other witnesses acquainted with the facts of the case, to execute a bond to appear before the magistrate for giving evidence in the matter of charge sheet laid against the accused. in such cases, the 'recognizance bond' in form 63 should be used. this is in accordance with sec. 170 cr.p.c.

a petty case register shall be maintained in all police stations in form 100 to enter cases booked under special and local laws like town nuisance act, mv act, ts gaming act where generally fine used to be imposed by courts. charge sheets in such cases are written on sheets in the same form.

the register maintained at the police station should be destroyed after 3 years. all petty cases come under the category of summary trial cases, the record available in the police station is the petty case register which should be properly maintained in form 100.
according to the telangana state police manual, the station house officer must have to maintain and to keep the following administrative records at police station..
1. fir index 2. nominal roll 3. duty roster 4. off duty roster 5. village roster 6. beat book

7. sample signatures book 8. point book 9. passport book 10. casual leave register 11. orderly room registers i & ii 12. court calender 13. visiting book of superior officers 14. visiting book of inspectors 15. arms deposit register 16. gun license register 17. arms register 18. note book of sis 29. book

a register must be kept in which entries must be made as and when cases with serial numbers are registered. this registration must be kept from year to year until all of the pages have been used. all disposals, such as filing charge sheets or closing cases (firs), should be done in red ink. if the case is charged and a court number is assigned, the particulars should be written; if the case is disposed of, the particulars should be shown with information, such as the ccno. and the date of disposition. the disposed of case numbers should be rounded off.

an up to date list of all personnel, including names and parentage, district number, date of birth, date of entry into service, and mobile number, should be neatly typed or written with their native village and district, educational qualifications, and the date of reporting at the police station, including the previous station, and kept handy at all times. if subordinates are not provided with government quarters, their residential addresses should be noted down with a pencil in urban or town police stations. the sho can use the nominal roll to provide any information related to the officers who work in a police station. special skills if any, should be noted in the nominal roll.
for the duties performed by the police station staff, a form 102 register must be kept. the names of the beats and the communities along the route

19. tappal book 20. small service book 21. certificate of appointment ( sanad) 22. quarterly kit inspection register 23. teliphone message register 24. line quarters register 25. un-claimed property register 26. informant sheet 27. telugu correspondence register 28. village vigilance committee

to the beats should be pasted to the first inner page of the duty roster, and the assorted beats should be lettered as a, b, c, and so on. in addition to regular duties such as investigation, process, village visits, guards, escorts, and other miscellaneous jobs, information regarding checking should be included in the register, and the number of men and officers, i.e. si, asi, should be listed underneath such duty columns. the sho's job is to make sure that the men's responsibilities are rotated in such a way that no one can complain.

according to the duty roster, an entry must be made in the general diary for the duties assigned. men should begin all tasks at the police station rather than at their homes. they must also return from duty and report to the police station before returning home.

a register showing the off duties given to the men should be maintained. once a week, men should take a clean day off for 24 hours. when the tasks for the next day are awarded, it is the sho's responsibility to announce the names and numbers of those who are given off the next day at the roll call. it is not required for him to be detailed a day off, but it is the sho's responsibility to ensure that every head constable and constable is given time off if not on the day allotted or on another date.

men are not entitled to extra duty allowance even though they are not assigned weekly duty, according to g.o.ms.no.l87 dated 29/06/1979, because they are allowed additional surrender leave for encashment.

a form 114 register should be kept at the rural police station. entries should be written in red ink for all of the villages in alphabetical order, noting the hamlets in blue or black ink beneath the villages to which they are linked. when the si, hc, or pc visits a particular village on any duty or specifically that village, an entry should be made in the village record with the date and number of the hc, pc, or si. for example, if the si visited "a" village on december 3, 2018, the entry should be as follows. a/si/ 03-122018. these entries should match the entries in the si or hc's notebook as well as the general diary. the si's responsibility is to visit every village and hamlet in a quarter, and if he is unable to do so, he must visit all of them in a half year. the purpose of this visit is to get local knowledge and establish relations with locals and officials.

the primary role of the police is to prevent and detect crime. it is essential that prevention takes precedence over detection. knowledge of the current state of affairs in the station house jurisdiction, as well as criminal movements and activities, are prerequisites to efficient crime prevention. an effective rhythm system is essential for gaining such knowledge.

a beat book in form 111 should be kept for every beat, including miscellaneous beats. constables on the beat should bring the book with them and record the information they gather in the proper columns. the signatures of at least two people whose sample signatures are kept in the station's sample signature book should be gathered in the appropriate columns by the beat constables.

the sho has to maintain a sample signatures book in the police station. in which the sample signatures of 4 or 5 residents at vulnerable locality in the a particular beat area shall be obtained every year for all the beats in the ps limits. the sho has to compare the signatures in the beat book which were obtained by the beat officer (blue colts) with the signatures taken in the sample signatures book to know the correctness of the beat report.

village officers or selected respectable residents of each village within the station limits should be given form 112 books. beat constables and checking officers who visit the village should sign the point books and note the date and time of their visit.

all head constables and constables leaving the station on duty or leave for treatment, including those traveling to the hospital for treatment, shall obtain a passport in form 104, except those who attend a hospital as outpatients. the superintendent of police or, in emergencies, the deputing officer must issue a passport in form 105 to any subordinate officers who are going on special duty outside the state. if the hc or pc who was on leave becomes ill, he should report to the next police station, surrender his existing passport, and acquire a new one for the hospital, if it is not possible to do so at his police station.

when such passports are issued by another ps's sho, he shall notify the concerned sho that an hc or pc was issued a sick passport and referred to such and such government hospital. if he wants to return to the hospital after his leave has expired, he must report to the local police station and obtain a new passport. after being released from the hospital, he should report to his assigned station or the station where he acquired his passport. a constable or head constable who is an inpatient and is absent from the hospital is subject to disciplinary action for absence.

the use of casual and compensatory leave by the subordinate officers at the station must be recorded in a register. the sho should not propose leave at random and without considering the issues and the number of guys on leave already. it's a good idea to set aside one sheet for each officer, with the officer's name, rank, and the amount of casual and compensatory leave he or she is eligible for during the year written on the top.

in a calendar year, a government employee is entitled to 15 days of unpaid leave. compensatory holidays of up to ten days are available to police subordinates below the rank of inspector within six months. casual and compensatory leave can be paired with a maximum of 10 days of vacation time. when casual or compensatory leave is approved, an entry will be made in the register, and the balance of leave will be shown. inspectors have the authority to award their subordinates compensatory or casual leave.

in an emergency, the sub-inspector or head constable in command of a station or outpost may award any of his subordinates casual or compensatory leave, as well as the ability to combine sundays and other allowed holidays with it. the matter may be reported to the inspector for confirmation within the stipulated limits.

metadata.subject: police_administration
metadata.heading: orderly room registers i and ii:

page_content: it is vital to produce the men in an orderly room as and when required to ensure discipline and timeliness. when they commit minor violations and errors, such as arriving late for work, failing to maintain proper public relations, lacking in knowledge and procedures, failing to write daily progress reports (dprs), and so on, the officer to whom the delinquent subordinates are presented should exercise powers with caution and serve more as a guide. it also aids in the resolution of men's legitimate issues. all representations/submissions from the men, such as cl, loan transfers, quarter allotment, and so on, should be treated in an orderly room as much as feasible. at the request of the men, orderly rooms can also be held. forms 161 and 162 are required to keep two separate registers for this.

a chart containing all the details of the case charged in court must be prepared at the police station. court numbers, dates of hearing, and pws examined so far, are to be examined, which is a convenient reference, so that the station house officer can get an idea of the court cases at a glance and pay attention to all such essential cases. if the number of cases charged is large, it is best to establish a separate schedule for each court.

whenever the superior officer visits or inspects the police station and, after going through the case files and records of the police station, enters his observations and directions in this book, a compliance report should be written in duplicate by the sho. the duplicate copy shall be retained in a separate file in the police station while the original is forwarded to the pertinent superior officer through the inspector. this book, which must be kept in form 104, is a permanent record.

a register must be kept so that the circle inspector who visits the station can write in his own hand on the station's records and files, as well as the directives to be followed by the sho. the compliance report to the inspector's notes must be kept in the same register, and an extract must be sent to the inspector.

at the police station level, a form 45 register must be kept, in which an entry must be made as and when the license holder deposits his or her arms. when a licensee's license was not renewed promptly, it should be returned to him upon his request. in case, a licensee has failed to renew his license in the prescribed time, the sho may seize the weapon during the license check. in those cases, the sho must likewise record a note in this register, hold the weapon in safe custody, and return it when the licensee presents his renewed license. the sho should report the deposit of guns at the police station to all concerned authorities.

for licenses given by the district magistrate under the arms act to anyone dwelling in the police station area, the station house officer should maintain an arms license register in form 'a'. similar registers are kept at the district magistrates office for licenses issued in the district, and at the mandal revenue officer's office for people who reside in that mandal. each town/village should be given enough pages to record the names of licensees, the details of the weapon, the license number, the ammunition authorized, renewals and checks, and the issuance of a new license to the same licensee for a different weapon.

the si should conduct surprise visits to the license holders' homes to inspect all licenses except revolvers and pistols. the revolvers and pistols must be checked by the inspector. if the sho holds the rank of inspector, he must inspect all weapons, including revolvers and pistols. weapons should be checked twice a year, in the first quarter and again in the third quarter. in the event of police stations located in agency areas with more than 300 gun licenses, the station's head constable may verify half the number of muzzle loader permits and half by the officer in charge of the police station once every six months. the officer in charge will review muzzle loader licenses for the second half of the year, which were previously reviewed by the head constable for the first half of the year, and the head constable will review muzzle loader licenses for the second half of the year, which were previously reviewed by the officer in charge for the first half of the year. only the person in charge should examine the remaining licenses.

these inspections should be recorded against appropriate entries, along with the results of the inspection. an abstract shall be created in form "b" at the end of each register, showing the number of each sort of license in the police station limits. at the start of each year, a new abstract should be sketched. in forms c and d, the district collector will send the details of licenses granted, renewed, or revoked during the month. no licensee should store a firearm without having it renewed. every quarter, this register must be compared to the mro office registration, either directly or by delegating this task to a subordinate.

each member of the force to whom the weapon is given is accountable for its maintenance and good condition, and a register must be kept in which information on the arms received in the station for the station staff and the allotment details to the staff is maintained. the sho is responsible for making sure that the weapons are cleaned regularly.

inspectors, sub-inspectors, and head constables should keep a notebook in which they must record their tasks, such as investigations conducted, places visited, information gathered, and watch on bad hats, etc., and also any special duties performed. these note books should be handed over to their successor whenever they are transferred, if not to the station house officer.

a book in form no. 106, in which entries about postage stamp accounts are to be kept. in addition to the inside and outward register, the second book of tappals sent and received must be kept. when tappals are presented to court clerks, it should be insisted on getting an acknowledgment from them as well.

every personnel below the level of si will be given small service books, on their enlistment into service. these books should be kept under lock and key with the station house officer, or another responsible officer in the case of other branches, and made available to the men as needed. entries should be created for the issue of kit pieces, incentives, and punishments given to them, as well as medical checks, trainings completed, transfers, and so on.

a constable is issued a certificate of appointment at the time of his appointment, in which entries are made regarding his name, parentage, and other details such as educational qualification, native place, and so on.

it is the responsibility of the station house officer to inspect the kits of all head constables and constables once a quarter, in the first week of each quarter, and if anyone is absent for that inspection, he should be asked to produce the kit later for inspection by sho, and a report should be submitted to the superintendent of police, requesting that the articles required for the men be sanctioned and that any shortages be distributed to them. small service books should be used to record entries.

a register for all telephone calls and messages received and transmitted from the police station should be maintained, and it should be kept in two different registers, one for messages received and one for messages sent from the police station with the date and time under the supervision of an educated pc. he should be trained how to properly answer calls and show respect to the person who is passing the message, whether it is an official or a private individual. the register should be placed before the sho immediately or as and when he returns in case he is away from the station. if the communication is important, the officer in charge of the station should act on it.

it is the responsibility of the sho in charge of the quarters and its premises to keep a register and ensure that they are maintained clean and tidy. the sho should visit these quarters regularly to inspect them, inquire about repairs from the tenants, and ensure that they are being attended to by the relevant department. the occupiers' names and dates of occupation and vacation should be recorded in this register.
this book will contain information about the unclaimed property, including who discovered it, when and where it was discovered, and how it was disposed of.

every police officer must have a source of information, and any confidential information gained must be kept confidential within the force. agents or sources must be known to the officers who employ them, as well as the information handed on to them. all information should be gathered without the exposure. no secret information, record, document, or information that leads or may lead to the disclosure of the identity of the source, agent, or information source, etc., or the disclosure of the identity of any police officer who is employed on any particular secret investigation shall be disclosed by any police officer to any other person unless the director general of police directs it.

no original or copy of a report containing confidential information acquired from an agent or source shall be transmitted. the information provided by the source or informant should be communicated to the appropriate person in a precise, summarised, or paraphrased form. the source or informant's identity should be kept secret at all times.

since telugu has been declared the official language of the state, it is the policy of the government that all departments, including the police department, communicate in telugu and keep a telugu language register, with a monthly return sent to the dpo through the proper channels. all records should be kept in telugu as well.

the sho should organize village vigilance committees in all of the villages and urge the youth and able bodied individuals of the village with a good reputation to join them. these representatives will serve as a vital link between the residents of the community and the police. crime is an important social concern, and crime prevention and detection will not progress without public cooperation.

before starting to organize or reorganize the committee, the inspector and sis should gain a good understanding of the village so that undesirable and unsuitable people are not included, as they cause more harm than good by assisting the police in the detection of crimes and apprehension of criminals.

the sho should keep a register with the names of all the people who are selected to be on the village vigilance committee, and each village should have its own set of pages. young and capable members of the village vigilance committee should organize a defensive team and assist in the organization of resistance to raids by any dacoits, robbers, or even naxalites. the sho and inspector should visit with these individuals as often as possible to ensure that a positive relationship is maintained and that prevention and detection are improved.
as per the provisions mentioned in the appm / tspm in pso-247-1, the following registers and sub-registers are kept for maintaining cash accounts.

- 1) cash book in aptc register no.5. - 2) printed receipt book in form no.5b. - 3) preliminary receipt book in form no.4. - 4) register of cheques, demand drafts, rbr draft forms etc. received in form no.14a.

- 1) permanent advance register (sr 5 tr 32 aptc) - 2) undisbursed pay register in form no.20. - 3) undisbursed acquittance register in form no.88. - 4) treasury bill book in form no.70. - 5) separate cash book in aptc form no.5 for non-departmental

a cashbook in aptc form 5 shall be maintained in every police station. it shall be a record of all cash transactions of the police station. all moneys received on government account, whether in cash, cheques, cash orders, remittance transfer receipts, or bills, which are convertible into cash, shall be brought to account by appropriate entries in the registers. for purposes of this order, bills other than pay bills signed by the superintendents of railway police, districts, and passed on to railway police inspectors for presentation at the treasuries with reference to instruction 3 (c) under treasury rule 19, shall be brought to account in the cash book immediately on receipt.

- a) the book shall contain two sets of pages the left hand pages are marked "dr" and the right hand pages "cr". - b) the permanent advance and all other moneys received on government account shall be entered on the "dr" side, and all disbursements or expenditure shall be entered on the ' cr' side. - c) to facilitate the easy check of disbursements, as against the corresponding receipts, the last column, viz., "head of account" on each half shall be used for noting the dates of disbursement or receipt of the different items. - d) sums paid to the personnel of other districts from the permanent advance and their recoupment shall be entered in red ink so that the adjustments of such end payments can be easily watched, and delays brought to notice of the concerned. - e) the book shall be closed at the end of each day on which there is a transaction, the cash on hand being shown, as the last entry for the day on the "cr" side and brought forward as the first entry of the next
day on the "dr" side.

- f) the book shall be in the custody of and shall be maintained by the sho personally. but when they are likely to be absent from the station or unit for a day or more the book may be handed over to the station writer. head constable or asst. sub-inspector with clear and definite instructions as to its maintenance. during the absence of the sho, the station writer hc or asi will be held personally responsible for the correct aintenance of the cashbook. when the sho returns to the station, he shall check the entries in the book and the cash balance and attest the same. - g) most of the police stations have a swhc. the duty of maintaining the cashbook may be generally delegated by the shos to him. this will not absolve the shos personal responsibility for the correctness of the cash and accounts. the sho should check the cash and accounts every day when in headquarters and attest the same. he should on his return check entries made in his absence, verify the cash and certify to the correctness of the balance in the cashbook. - h) the cash on hand in each station shall be kept in a locked drawer or other suitable receptacle. when charge of the cashbook is handed over, cash balance together with the key of the receptacle containing the cash shall be handed over on record in the general diary, the amount handed over being stated in words and figures. - i) the sdpo shall, when at his headquarters, check the entries in the book maintained in his headquarters police station daily, if possible, and in any no be circumstances not less than once a week. on return after an absence from his headquarters, he shall examine the book and check and initial the transactions curing his absence. in respect of cash books maintained in other stations of his subdivision, the sdpo shall check the book of each station as often as possible during his visits and not less than once a month. he should certify in the cash bee 260 on book and mention in his diary that he has done so, that the books are in order of any that the cash balance is correct, that all payments from time permanent advance up to the date of the submission of the last contingent and travelling allowance bills have been claimed and that full cash recoveries have been made in respect of all bills cashed up-to-date. - j) at the end of every month a detailed balance sheet in form 34 shall be made out in the cashbook in the manner prescribed in order 246.

metadata.subject: police_administration
metadata.heading: station house officers to send contingent bills

page_content: (pso-248) each sho of police station/ri shall submit to the district police office / unit office by the 20 th of the month a bill in form 35 for the contingent charges of his station. the bill should include all fixed charges, such as rent, electricity, water, telephones and wages of the contingent staff and also the expenditure actually incurred up to the date of submission of the bill under a fluctuating items, such as postal stamp charge for which vouchers should be attached. for any charges of an unusual nature, the previous sanction of the sp should be obtained through the regular channel before the amount is included in the bill. the sdpo shall make out a separate contingent bill for the charges incurred by him and forward it to the district police office by the 20 th of the month. these bills will be checked in the district police office and passed by the superintendent of police.
apart from the statutory, crime, administrative records, the police station is also mandated to maintain the following miscellaneous records:
for all stations where standing guards are allocated, printed orders in english, telugu and urdu shall be displayed in guardrooms along with the following details.

- 1) the guards' sanctioned strength. - 2) the number of firearms and ammunition that the guard will be given. - 3) the number of personnel who must be present during the day and night. - 4) the number of sentries that must be stationed during the day and night, together with their exact location, beats, detailed duties, and responsibilities. - 5) arms are to be carried by sentries at all times of the day and night. - 6) the sentry periods change. - 7) the posting and relief of sentries. - 8) keeping a sentry relief book up to date.

- 9) the premises and articles for which the sentry is responsible. - 10) the guard in-charge officer's responsibilities. - 11) an order to report to the sho right away. - 12) a directive to quickly notify the sho of any significant event. - 13) the sho or an officer of equal rank, such as a crime inspector, inspects the guard. - 14) a warning that failure to perform one's duties would result in serious consequences.

all guards must keep a sentry relief book in good order. when a sentry is replaced, the guard officer and the relieving sentry shall sign the sentry relief book. the guard officer must record all details such as any guard member's absence or neglect of duty, any prisoner's escape, the fact that the treasury seal was correctly received and handed over, the letting out of prisoners from their cells for any reason, their return to cells, and all other guard duties. officers who are monitoring the guard should keep track of their visits and details in the sentry relief book.

the police department is allowed to use memorandum-style correspondence by the government. for any official correspondence, the sho should keep a memo form book in form 28. the station house officer must keep separate memo form books for criminal and administrative concerns.

the district armed reserve force will be mobilized once a year to keep it in good shape, and its standing tasks should be taken over by civil police during that time. standing orders should be set up for this purpose, and a copy should be displayed in every police station.
the orders should include details such as
- 1) the number of men of each level to be dispatched. - 2) their duties. - 3) the arms and ammunition they should bring, and so on. (the men should bring their entire kit.)

if the mobilization lasts 15 days, the men should be replaced once a day by substitutes from the same circle. when drafting mobilization orders that influence vital civil police activities, caution should be exercised.

to provide essential co-operation with income tax officers, all station house officers should keep a list of wealthy persons in their station bounds on form 53. shos can conduct private inquiries regarding criminals' wealth during their routine trips to villages.

an information book should be kept in each functionally divided law and order station with all of the material found in parts iv of the station crime history. it should include information about factions and conflicts, castes and communal tensions, major festivals, antisocial individuals in the area, public resorts, and so on. in a nutshell, all information that could be relevant to a new station officer should be recorded in the register.

page_content: - 1) police gazettes. - 2) gazettes of criminal intelligence. - 3) sheets of crimes and occurrence - 4) general memorandums - 5) the circular of superintendent of police. - 6) memos from the inspector and the sdpo. - 7) memorandums of the magistrate. - 8) copy of sections 30, 30-a, and 31 of the police act, 1861 - 9) subject-wise sb folders.

the sho must provide a list of absconding warrantees and ov criminals to the district crime bureau, along with the individuals' descriptive details from form 125. these lists are referred to as 'a' lists and will be published as a supplement to the 'crime occurrence sheet.' these 'a' lists should be filed separately in each station, and a manuscript list should be hung in every police station so that personnel can see the absconding persons' details. the names of people who have been arrested or whose arrest is no longer necessary will be published in the c & 0 sheet as the 'b' list.

page_content: the station crime history part-i to v provides information on crime and criminals. the station crime history is a record of crimes and criminals who committed various types of property crimes in that particular police station area, excluding cases of criminal breach of trust or misappropriation, criminal trespass or house trespass causing bodily harm, and criminals who lived in the police station area but committed crimes in other police station limits. sections 378 to 462 of the indian penal code deal with property crimes.

this register is also known as the crime, occurrence, and classification register. all true cases registered in the ps (including attempts) shall be entered in the order of their occurrence coming, under the following (179) heads of crime, which are listed alphabetically. the necessity to reclassify indicated below, it should also be entered. there is no harm in first entering the method as found in the crime code book and adding the other classifications applicable to the case from the list given below. the crime details which are ascertained after preliminary investigation and which are incorporated in the crime details form 76 should be entered in column 9 along with the other information. the details about the criminal or suspect should also conform to the code book while making entries in the columns relating to accused or suspect. the existing classification in the state, which is updated, is retained and should be followed in all entries to be made in the registers.
